name: Staff Handbook & Policies
purpose: Creates a comprehensive, accessible staff handbook that codifies the cafe's expectations, policies, procedures, and cultural standards into a single reference document. This block ensures every team member — from day-one starters to long-serving staff — has a clear, written source of truth for how the cafe operates and what is expected of them.

detailed_objectives: Draft a complete staff handbook covering employment terms, conduct expectations, health and safety basics, and day-to-day operational policies
Ensure all policies comply with UK employment law including the Employment Rights Act 1996, Equality Act 2010, and Working Time Regulations 1998
Create a document that is genuinely useful and readable — not a legalistic tome that sits in a drawer unread
Establish clear disciplinary and grievance procedures that protect both the business and the employee
Provide a signed acknowledgement process so the cafe can demonstrate every team member has received and understood the handbook
secondary_objectives: Reduce workplace disputes by setting clear expectations before problems arise
Support the cafe's defence in any employment tribunal claim by demonstrating documented policies were communicated
Create a cultural document that reflects the cafe's values and personality, not just legal minimums

why_this_matters: Most small cafes operate without a staff handbook, relying instead on verbal instructions, assumed knowledge, and the owner's memory of what was agreed. This works until it does not — and when it fails, the consequences are severe. Without written policies, a cafe owner has no documented defence if an employee claims unfair dismissal at an employment tribunal. Under UK law, employers with any number of staff must provide a written statement of employment particulars from day one (Employment Rights Act 1996, updated April 2020), and failure to do so can result in tribunal awards of two to four weeks' pay. Beyond legal compliance, the absence of a handbook creates daily operational friction. Staff argue about who said what regarding holiday requests. A barista claims they were never told about the social media policy after posting a negative comment online. A team member accuses the manager of inconsistent treatment because there is no documented standard to measure against. Every one of these disputes could have been prevented with a clear, written reference. A good staff handbook is not bureaucracy — it is the foundation upon which fair, consistent management is built. It tells every team member exactly where they stand, protects the owner when difficult decisions must be made, and sets the cultural tone for how people treat each other at work. For a cafe serving the public, where reputation is everything, the handbook is the invisible infrastructure of professionalism.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Gather all existing written materials — employment contracts, offer letters, any informal policy notes, health and safety notices, and the written statement of employment particulars you are legally required to issue on day one. Audit what you already have against a basic handbook checklist: company overview, employment terms, hours and attendance, absence reporting, holiday entitlement, dress code, health and safety, data protection basics, social media policy, disciplinary procedure, and grievance procedure. Most small cafes will find they have fragments but no cohesive document. Week 2: Draft Section 1 — Welcome and Company Overview. This is where you set the tone. Write a brief welcome from the owner explaining the cafe's story, values, and what makes it a good place to work. Include the cafe's mission statement if you have one (or write one now — even two sentences will do). Then draft Section 2 — Employment Basics: probation period, notice periods, pay arrangements, tips policy, hours of work, overtime, and time recording. Use plain English throughout — avoid legal jargon. The goal is a document that a 17-year-old starting their first job can read and understand. Print and distribute what you have so far, even if incomplete, with a clear note that remaining sections are coming. Getting something into people's hands quickly is better than waiting months for a perfect document.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Complete the remaining handbook sections systematically. Section 3 — Conduct and Standards: personal appearance, uniform requirements, mobile phone use, social media policy, timekeeping, and general behaviour expectations. Section 4 — Absence and Leave: how to report sickness (who to call, by what time), return-to-work conversations, holiday booking procedure, and other leave types (compassionate, jury service, medical appointments). Section 5 — Health, Safety, and Wellbeing: key responsibilities, accident reporting, fire evacuation, manual handling basics, and where to find the full H&S policy. Section 6 — Disciplinary and Grievance: a step-by-step disciplinary procedure (informal warning, first written warning, final written warning, dismissal) that follows the ACAS Code of Practice, plus a grievance procedure allowing staff to raise concerns formally. This section is critical — failure to follow a fair procedure is the most common reason small businesses lose at tribunal. Section 7 — Data Protection: a brief GDPR statement covering how you handle employee data, CCTV use, and staff right to access their records. Once all sections are complete, format the handbook cleanly with a contents page and page numbers. Create an acknowledgement page at the back for the team member to sign, confirming they have received, read, and understood the handbook. Issue the complete handbook to every team member and file the signed acknowledgements securely.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). The handbook is now a living document, not a one-off project. Schedule a formal review every 12 months — mark the date in the calendar now — to update policies for any changes in law, business practice, or team feedback. Each time you update the handbook, reissue it to all staff and collect fresh acknowledgement signatures. Integrate the handbook into the onboarding process so every new starter receives a copy on their first day and has a guided walk-through of key sections during induction week. Consider creating a quick-reference card or poster for the staff area covering the five most commonly referenced policies (absence reporting, holiday booking, dress code, social media, disciplinary stages). Solicit staff feedback on the handbook during annual reviews — are there areas that are unclear, sections that feel outdated, or topics that should be added? The best handbooks evolve based on real workplace questions. For chain or franchise cafes, align the handbook with any group-level policies while adding site-specific content. Store a digital copy accessible to all staff (a shared drive or simple app) alongside printed copies in the staff area.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Audit all existing written employment documents and policy fragments
Draft Section 1 (Welcome and Company Overview) with owner's welcome message
Draft Section 2 (Employment Basics) covering pay, hours, probation, and notice periods
Create a handbook checklist tracking which sections are complete and which remain
Issue draft sections to all current staff with a timeline for completion
Ensure all staff have received a written statement of employment particulars as required by law

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Draft remaining sections: Conduct, Absence, Health & Safety, Disciplinary & Grievance, Data Protection
Ensure the disciplinary procedure follows the ACAS Code of Practice
Format the complete handbook with contents page, page numbers, and professional layout
Create a signed acknowledgement page and issue to all team members
File all signed acknowledgements securely with employee records
Brief the team on key policies during a dedicated team meeting
Integrate handbook issuance into the new starter onboarding process

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Schedule and conduct an annual handbook review to update for legal changes and business developments
Reissue updated handbooks with fresh acknowledgement signatures each year
Create a quick-reference card or poster for the staff area covering the five most referenced policies
Solicit staff feedback on handbook clarity and completeness during annual reviews
Maintain a digital copy accessible to all staff alongside printed versions in the staff area
Review and update the handbook immediately whenever a significant policy change occurs mid-year

deliverables: Quick-Reference Policy Card for the staff area

tools.description: Complete handbook template with pre-structured sections for company overview, employment terms, conduct standards, absence policies, health and safety, disciplinary and grievance procedures, and data protection. Written in plain English with cafe-specific examples.
One-page sign-off form for each team member confirming receipt, reading, and understanding of the staff handbook. Includes date, printed name, signature, and a space for any questions raised.

Step-by-step disciplinary procedure following the ACAS Code of Practice, covering informal discussion, first written warning, final written warning, and dismissal. Includes a parallel grievance procedure for staff to raise formal concerns.
Laminated A4 card for display in the staff area summarising the five most frequently referenced policies: absence reporting, holiday booking, dress code, social media, and disciplinary stages.

Yearly audit checklist ensuring every handbook section is reviewed against current UK employment law, updated for any business changes, and reissued to all staff with fresh acknowledgement signatures.

failure_modes: Handbook is written in impenetrable legal language that no one reads, providing theoretical compliance but zero practical value
Policies are documented but never enforced consistently, creating a worse situation than having no handbook at all because staff can claim selective enforcement
Acknowledgement signatures are collected but the handbook is never actually explained, leaving staff unaware of its contents when issues arise
Handbook is created once and never reviewed, gradually drifting out of compliance with evolving employment law
kpis: 100% of current staff have signed handbook acknowledgement on file
100% of new starters receive handbook and sign acknowledgement within first week
Annual handbook review completed within 30 days of scheduled date
Zero employment tribunal claims citing lack of documented policies
Staff can correctly identify key policies (absence reporting, disciplinary stages) when asked
constraints: Disciplinary and grievance procedures must comply with the ACAS Code of Practice — deviation can result in tribunal uplift awards of up to 25%
Written statement of employment particulars is a legal requirement from day one under the Employment Rights Act 1996
Handbook must be reviewed by someone with HR or employment law knowledge before finalisation to avoid inadvertently creating unenforceable or illegal policies

time_load.L1: 6 hours (audit existing documents, draft first two sections, distribute)
time_load.L2: 10 hours (draft remaining sections, format, brief team, collect acknowledgements)
time_load.L3: 3 hours/year (annual review, update, reissue, collect new signatures)

review_protocol: Full handbook review annually or whenever UK employment law changes significantly. Immediate review of specific sections after any workplace incident that reveals a policy gap. Spot-check during staff one-to-ones that team members know where to find the handbook and can recall key policies.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block creates a complete, readable staff handbook covering employment terms, conduct expectations, absence rules, health and safety, disciplinary procedures, and data protection. It protects the business legally and gives every team member a clear reference for how things work.
implementation_risks: Owners may attempt to draft policies without HR or legal input and inadvertently create unenforceable terms
Handbook can become a 'shelfware' document that nobody references after initial issuance
Overly restrictive policies (e.g. blanket social media bans) can damage culture and feel disproportionate
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate when signals indicate missing employment documentation, inconsistent policy application, staff disputes about rules, or preparation for scaling the team. This is foundational — most D12 blocks assume a handbook exists.
